text:
Summarize (Orchestrating the Discussion)

Discussing Solutions and Strategies

Have students present and discuss their strategies for answering the questions. Focus the students on the equivalent ratio and unit rate relationships.

Suggested Questions

With each strategy in the Initial Challenge, have students justify why the strategy works. Then ask students to connect how the different strategies represent the same type of thinking.

  • Whose strategy (Brandon's, Sofia's, or Kaedon's) is most like how you thought about answering the questions about the smoothie bars?
  • Is there one strategy you prefer over the others? Why?
  • How do the strategies compare?
  • How did you fill in your rate table for What If..? Situation A?
  • How is finding equivalent ratios helpful to answer questions?
  • How does finding the quantities on the table help you think about how to write the equation for the cost in What If..? Situation A?

• Answers will vary.
  • Answers will vary. All the strategies work and provide different levels of efficiency. It would be interesting to note if students become flexible at using different strategies at different times.
  • Students should notice that all three strategies are related to ratio relationships. Sofia is scaling the ratios. Brandon scales to find a unit rate that is easily scaled to other quantities. Kaedon represent the unit rate algebraically. He can then use that expression or equation to find other equivalent quantities.
  • Some students might discuss making jumps (multiplicative reasoning using factors and multiples to find equivalent ratios). Some students might discuss finding the unit rate and scaling the unit rate to find equivalent ratios.
  • Finding different amounts of the variables answers the questions. You can use equivalent ratios to find equivalent variable amounts.
  • The repeated reasoning in the table helps to find the pattern that can be expressed as a rule. That rule can be written as an equation.
